1→# Architecture: web-tml24c
     2→
     3→## Decision: Rust/Yew with cor24-rs library (not Emscripten)
     4→
     5→### Approach
     6→
     7→Use **cor24-rs** (assembler + emulator) as a Rust library dependency. The tml24c
     8→C interpreter is pre-compiled to COR24 assembly via tc24r, then assembled and
     9→executed in the browser-based COR24 emulator. Lisp I/O flows through the
    10→emulated UART.
    11→
    12→### Why not Emscripten?
    13→
    14→Compiling tml24c C code directly to WASM via Emscripten would work but defeats
    15→the purpose: users want to see Lisp running *on COR24*. The cor24-rs emulator
    16→provides instruction tracing, register visibility, and memory inspection — all
    17→essential for the Phase 3 execution trace feature.
    18→
    19→### Why not a Rust rewrite of the Lisp core?
    20→
    21→Unnecessary. The C code compiles to COR24 via tc24r already. A Rust rewrite
    22→would duplicate effort and diverge from the canonical tml24c implementation.
    23→
    24→## Pipeline
    25→
    26→```
    27→Build time:
    28→  tml24c (C) --[tc24r]--> tml24c.s (COR24 assembly)
    29→
    30→Browser runtime:
    31→  tml24c.s --[cor24-rs assembler]--> binary
    32→  binary   --[cor24-rs emulator]-->  running COR24 CPU
    33→  User Lisp input --> UART RX --> interpreter --> UART TX --> output display
    34→```
    35→
    36→## Stack
    37→
    38→- **Frontend**: Yew (Rust WASM framework), same as cor24-rs
    39→- **Build tool**: Trunk (WASM bundler)
    40→- **CPU/Assembler**: cor24-emulator crate (path dependency to cor24-rs)
    41→- **ISA types**: cor24-isa crate
    42→
    43→## Project Structure
    44→
    45→```
    46→web-tml24c/
    47→├── Cargo.toml          # Workspace root
    48→├── Trunk.toml          # WASM build config
    49→├── index.html          # Entry point
    50→├── src/
    51→│   ├── main.rs         # Yew app entry
    52→│   └── repl.rs         # REPL component
    53→├── docs/
    54→│   └── architecture.md # This file
    55→└── asm/
    56→    └── tml24c.s        # Pre-compiled Lisp interpreter (from tc24r)
    57→```
    58→
    59→## Component Design
    60→
    61→### REPL Component
    62→
    63→- Text input area for Lisp expressions
    64→- Eval button (sends input to emulated UART)
    65→- Output area (reads from emulated UART output buffer)
    66→- The COR24 emulator runs the tml24c interpreter binary
    67→- Each eval: write Lisp text to UART RX, run CPU until output appears on UART TX
    68→
    69→### Future: Execution Trace (Phase 3)
    70→
    71→The cor24-rs `TraceBuffer` provides a ring buffer of the last 200 executed
    72→instructions with before/after register state. This will power the visual
    73→execution trace showing how the Lisp evaluator steps through COR24 instructions.
    74→

content.content: -rw-r--r--@   1 mike  staff  6455 Mar 23 17:54 README.md

1→# tc24r -- Tiny C Compiler for the COR24 FPGA Soft CPU
     2→
     3→tc24r is an open-source C compiler targeting the **COR24** (C-Oriented RISC, 24-bit) instruction set architecture. COR24 is an FPGA soft CPU designed by [MakerLisp](https://makerlisp.com) for efficient C code execution. The existing MakerLisp C compiler (cc24) is proprietary -- tc24r provides an open-source alternative.
     4→
     5→The approach is inspired by [chibicc](https://github.com/rui314/chibicc), a small educational C compiler. tc24r follows the same architecture pattern (recursive-descent parser, direct assembly emission) but is written from scratch in Rust.
     6→
     7→## Project Status
     8→
     9→tc24r is **functional** -- it compiles real C programs to COR24 assembly that runs on hardware and the cor24-rs emulator. 14 components, ~50 crates, 46 demos all passing. See [docs/status.md](docs/status.md) for detailed status and test counts.
    10→
    11→### What Works
    12→
    13→- Types: int (24-bit), unsigned int, char (8-bit), void, pointers, arrays
    14→- All standard C operators with correct precedence
    15→- Control flow: if/else, while, do...while, for, break, continue, switch/case
    16→- Ternary operator (? :), character literals, multi-declaration
    17→- Prefix/postfix increment and decrement (++i, i--)
    18→- Compound assignment: +=, -=, *=, /=, %=, &=, |=, ^=, <<=, >>=
    19→- sizeof, typedef, enum, struct (dot and arrow access), union
    20→- Function prototypes (forward declarations, mutual recursion)
    21→- Functions with multiple parameters, recursion, ISR support
    22→- Globals, string constants, hex literals
    23→- Pointer arithmetic with element-size scaling
    24→- Preprocessor: #define, #include, #pragma once
    25→- Inline assembly: asm("...")
    26→- MMIO: LED, UART TX/RX, interrupt enable
    27→- Software division and modulo runtime
    28→
    29→### What Does Not Work Yet
    30→
    31→- Multi-file compilation
    32→- float/double (COR24 has no FPU -- out of scope)
    33→
    34→## COR24 Architecture at a Glance
    35→
    36→- **24-bit** registers and address space (16 MB)
    37→- **8 registers**: r0-r2 (general purpose), fp, sp, z (zero), iv, ir
    38→- **32 instructions**: arithmetic, logic, shift, compare, branch, load/store, stack ops
    39→- **1/2/4-byte** variable-length instruction encoding
    40→- Hardware multiply (24-cycle), no hardware divide
    41→- Little-endian, byte-addressable memory
    42→- UART and GPIO via memory-mapped I/O
    43→
    44→## Component Structure
    45→
    46→The compiler is organized into 14 components with ~50 crates, each component
    47→being its own Cargo workspace under `components/`:
    48→
    49→| Component | Purpose |
    50→|-----------|---------|
    51→| core | Shared types: AST, tokens, spans, errors, traits |
    52→| frontend | Preprocessing, lexing, parsing |
    53→| backend | Legacy codegen and validation |
    54→| codegen-emit | Assembly emission (core, data, load/store) |
    55→| codegen-expr | Expression codegen (call, literal, ops, pointer, variable) |
    56→| codegen-ops | Operator codegen (arithmetic, bitwise, compare, divmod, incdec, logical, unary, type-infer) |
    57→| codegen-state | Codegen state management |
    58→| codegen-stmt | Statement codegen (control flow, simple statements) |
    59→| codegen-structure | Function structure (ISR, locals, prologue) |
    60→| config | Configuration and target definition |
    61→| dispatch | Codegen dispatch layer |
    62→| macros | Proc macros (asm-dsl, emit-macros, handler-macros) |
    63→| testing | Test harnesses (as24, compile, cor24, golden) |
    64→| cli | CLI binary entry point |
    65→
    66→See [docs/architecture.md](docs/architecture.md) for data flow and design constraints.
    67→
    68→## Building
    69→
    70→Build all components:
    71→
    72→```bash
    73→scripts/build-all.sh
    74→```
    75→
    76→This runs each component's `scripts/build.sh` in dependency order. The release binary is produced at:
    77→
    78→```
    79→components/cli/target/release/tc24r
    80→```
    81→
    82→To build a single component:
    83→
    84→```bash
    85→cargo build --manifest-path components/<name>/Cargo.toml
    86→```
    87→
    88→## Usage
    89→
    90→```bash
    91→tc24r <input.c> [-o output.s] [-I dir]
    92→```
    93→
    94→- `<input.c>` -- C source file to compile
    95→- `-o output.s` -- output assembly file (default: stdout)
    96→- `-I dir` -- add include search path (repeatable)
    97→
    98→### Full Workflow
    99→
   100→```bash

Now I have enough information. Let me create a comprehensive summary of the project exploration:

## Comprehensive Project Exploration Summary

I've conducted a thorough exploration of the web-tml24c project and its related dependencies. Here's what I found:

### 1. **Current State of src/ - Rust/Yew Code Structure**

The project has minimal but complete Rust code:

**Files:**
- `/Users/mike/github/sw-vibe-coding/web-tml24c/src/main.rs` - Entry point that sets up panic hook and renders the App component
- `/Users/mike/github/sw-vibe-coding/web-tml24c/src/lib.rs` - Root component wrapping the Repl
- `/Users/mike/github/sw-vibe-coding/web-tml24c/src/repl.rs` - Full REPL implementation (282 lines)

**Repl.rs Architecture:**
- Uses `cor24_emulator::Assembler` and `EmulatorCore` from the cor24-rs library
- Includes the pre-compiled tml24c assembly at build time via `include_str!("../asm/tml24c.s")`
- `Msg` enum handles: Init, Tick, InputChanged, Eval
- Component lifecycle:
  - `create()`: Initializes EmulatorCore, queues Init message
  - `update()` handles initialization: assembles code, loads into emulator, runs tests
  - Tick loop: runs 50,000 instruction batches, reads UART output, reschedules via gloo timers
  - Eval: sends input bytes to UART RX, resumes execution
- UI: Two-panel layout (output above, input below) with split-pane styling, inline CSS with theming

### 2. **Build Configuration**

**Cargo.toml** - Edition 2024, key dependencies:
- `cor24-emulator = { path = "../../sw-embed/cor24-rs" }` - Path dependency to assembler/emulator
- `cor24-isa = { path = "../../sw-embed/cor24-rs/isa", features = ["serde"] }` - ISA definitions
- `yew = { version = "0.21", features = ["csr"] }` - Client-side rendering
- `wasm-bindgen = "0.2"` - WASM FFI
- `web-sys` - DOM/console access
- `gloo = "0.11"` - Web utilities (timers, storage, net)
- `console_error_panic_hook` - Error reporting

**Trunk.toml:**
- Output directory: `pages` (dist folder for built artifacts)
- Dev server port: 7402

**index.html:**
- Catppuccin dark theme color scheme (mocha palette)
- Monospace font stack (JetBrains Mono, Fira Code, Cascadia Code)
- Root `#app` div for Yew to mount
- Includes `<link data-trunk rel="rust" data-bin="web-tml24c" />` for Trunk

**Build Status:** ✅ Both `cargo check` and `trunk build` complete successfully. The WASM bundle exists in `/Users/mike/github/sw-vibe-coding/web-tml24c/pages/` (5.1 MB WASM, 48 KB JS, 2.2 KB HTML).

### 3. **Architecture Documentation**

**/Users/mike/github/sw-vibe-coding/web-tml24c/docs/architecture.md** (74 lines) documents:

**Decision: Rust/Yew + cor24-rs library** (not Emscripten, not Rust rewrite):
- Rationale: Use cor24-rs as a library dependency; pre-compile tml24c C code to COR24 assembly via tc24r, then assemble and execute in the browser emulator
- Why not Emscripten: Defeats the purpose — users want to see Lisp running *on COR24*, not native WASM
- Why not Rust rewrite: Unnecessary; C code already compiles via tc24r

**Pipeline:**
```
Build time: tml24c (C) --[tc24r]--> tml24c.s (COR24 assembly)
Runtime: tml24c.s --[assembler]--> binary --[emulator]--> running CPU
         User input --> UART RX --> Lisp interpreter --> UART TX --> output
```

**Stack:**
- Frontend: Yew (Rust WASM framework)
- Build tool: Trunk
- CPU: cor24-emulator crate
- ISA: cor24-isa crate

**Future:** Phase 3 will use the cor24-rs `TraceBuffer` for visual execution traces.

### 4. **cor24-emulator Library Exports (cor24-rs)**

**Location:** `/Users/mike/github/sw-embed/cor24-rs/src/emulator.rs` (500+ lines)

**EmulatorCore public API:**
- **Loading:** `load_lgo()`, `load_program()`, `hard_reset()`, `reset()`
- **Execution:** `run_batch(max_instructions)` → `BatchResult { reason: StopReason, instructions_run, uart_bytes_added, led_changed }`
- **StopReason enum:** CycleLimit, Halted, InvalidInstruction, Breakpoint, Paused
- **Control:** `resume()`, `pause()`, `step()`, `step_over()`
- **Breakpoints:** `add_breakpoint()`, `remove_breakpoint()`, `clear_breakpoints()`
- **State queries:** `snapshot()` → CpuSnapshot (registers, PC, condition flag, cycle count, instruction count, LED, UART output length)
- **Memory access:** `read_byte()`, `write_byte()`, `read_word()`, `read_memory()`, `sram()`, `ebr()`
- **I/O:** `get_led()`, `set_button_pressed()`, `get_uart_output()`, `send_uart_byte()`, `clear_uart_output()`
- **Disassembly:** `disassemble_at(addr)` → (mnemonic_string, size_in_bytes)
- **Tracing:** `trace()` → TraceBuffer (last 200 instructions with register state)

**Assembler API:**
- `Assembler::new()`, `assemble(source: &str)` → `AssemblyResult`
- AssemblyResult contains: `bytes`, `lines` (with addresses and source), `errors`, `labels` (HashMap)

**CpuState:** 8 registers (r0-r2 general, r3=fp, r4=sp, r5=z, r6=iv, r7=ir), 24-bit, 16MB address space, little-endian

### 5. **tml24c C Source (Tiny Macro Lisp)**

**Location:** `/Users/mike/github/sw-vibe-coding/tml24c/src/` (1,126 lines across 8 header files + 1 C file)

**Files:**
- `tml.h` - Core types (49 lines): Tag system (Fixnum, Cons, Symbol, Extended), heap size 4096 cells
- `io.h` - UART/LED I/O (40 lines)
- `heap.h` - Heap memory management (42 lines)
- `symbol.h` - Symbol table (74 lines)
- `print.h` - S-expression printer (35 lines)
- `read.h` - S-expression reader (158 lines)
- `eval.h` - Evaluator (333 lines): quote, if, define, lambda, defmacro, begin; 18 primitives
- `gc.h` - Garbage collection (150 lines)
- `main.c` - Tests and REPL loop (245 lines)

**Tag System (2-bit encoding):**
```
[23..2] payload  [1:0] tag
00  Fixnum (22-bit signed)
01  Cons (heap cell pointer)
10  Symbol (symbol table index)
11  Extended (heap cell, holds closures/macros/primitives/strings)
```

**Core Primitives (18):**
- Arithmetic: +, -, *, /, %
- Comparison: <, =
- List ops: cons, car, cdr, list
- Type checks: null?, pair?, atom?, number?, eq?
- Logic: not
- I/O: print

**Special Forms (6):**
- quote, if, define, lambda, defmacro, begin

**Capabilities:**
- S-expression reader (handles integers, symbols, lists, dotted pairs, quote shorthand)
- Recursive evaluator with lexical environments
- Closures and macros
- Tests cover: scaffold, reader, and evaluator

### 6. **tc24r C-to-COR24 Compiler**

**Location:** `/Users/mike/github/sw-vibe-coding/tc24r/` (git repository)

**Status:** Functional — compiles real C programs. 14 components, ~50 crates, 46 demos, all passing.

**What Works:**
- Types: int (24-bit), unsigned int, char (8-bit), void, pointers, arrays
- All C operators with correct precedence
- Control flow: if/else, while, do...while, for, break, continue, switch/case
- Functions with parameters, recursion, ISR support
- Globals, string constants, hex literals
- Preprocessor: #define, #include, #pragma once
- Inline assembly: asm("...")
- MMIO: LED, UART TX/RX, interrupt control
- sizeof, typedef, enum, struct, union

**Component Structure:**
- 14 components under `components/`: core, frontend, backend, codegen-emit, codegen-expr, codegen-ops, codegen-state, codegen-stmt, codegen-structure, config, dispatch, macros, testing, cli
- Release binary: `components/cli/target/release/tc24r`
- Usage: `tc24r <input.c> [-o output.s] [-I dir]`

**tml24c.s (Pre-compiled):**
- Located: `/Users/mike/github/sw-vibe-coding/web-tml24c/asm/tml24c.s` (20,308 lines, 386 KB)
- Generated by tc24r from tml24c C source
- Contains entry point (_start), function definitions, including putc_uart, puts_str, and the full Lisp interpreter

### 7. **Project Roadmap (AgentRail Saga)**

The project follows AgentRail methodology with 5 planned steps:

1. **001-research-and-scaffold** (✅ COMPLETED)
   - Researched dependencies, decided on architecture
   - Created Rust/Yew project structure
   - Integrated cor24-rs and asm/tml24c.s
   - Builds successfully

2. **002-lisp-wasm** (🟡 IN PROGRESS)
   - Get Lisp evaluator running in WASM

3. **003-repl**
   - Interactive REPL component

4. **004-examples**
   - Example Lisp programs

5. **005-trace**
   - Visual execution trace visualization

### 8. **Key Integration Points**

**How everything connects:**

1. **C → Assembly:** tml24c C code compiled to tml24c.s (COR24 assembly) via tc24r
2. **Assembly → Binary:** Trunk build-time `include_str!("../asm/tml24c.s")` embeds assembly
3. **Assembly → WASM:** Repl::update(Init) calls Assembler::assemble() on embedded ASM
4. **Binary → Emulation:** Bytes loaded into EmulatorCore memory at address 0
5. **User → UART:** Text input sent byte-by-byte to UART RX via send_uart_byte()
6. **UART → Output:** Tick loop reads emulator's uart_output string continuously

**Build Pipeline:**
```
tc24r compiles tml24c.c → tml24c.s
Include tml24c.s in Cargo build
trunk build Rust+WASM
cargo compiles web-tml24c+cor24-rs to WASM
Output: pages/index.html + .wasm + .js
```

### 9. **Current Build Status**

✅ **Fully working:**
- Cargo check: Pass (9.34s)
- Trunk build: Pass (produces pages/web-tml24c-*.wasm, .js, index.html)
- WASM artifact size: 5.1 MB (will compress to ~1-2 MB with gzip)
- Both edition 2024 Rust configurations in place

### 10. **Technical Summary**

**The System:**
This is a sophisticated polyglot architecture combining C Lisp interpreter, Rust/Yew UI, COR24 emulator, and a custom C-to-COR24 compiler. Users write Lisp expressions in the browser, which are evaluated by the pre-compiled tml24c interpreter running in an emulated COR24 CPU, with all I/O flowing through the emulated UART to the web UI.

**What's Ready:**
- Full REPL UI with proper styling
- EmulatorCore fully integrated and operational
- Assembly loading and CPU execution working
- UART I/O plumbing complete

## Comprehensive Project Exploration Summary

I've conducted a thorough exploration of the web-tml24c project and its related dependencies. Here's what I found:

### 1. **Current State of src/ - Rust/Yew Code Structure**

The project has minimal but complete Rust code:

**Files:**

- `/Users/mike/github/sw-vibe-coding/web-tml24c/src/repl.rs` - Full REPL implementation (282 lines)

**Repl.rs Architecture:**
- Uses `cor24_emulator::Assembler` and `EmulatorCore` from the cor24-rs library
- Includes the pre-compiled tml24c assembly at build time via `include_str!("../asm/tml24c.s")`
- `Msg` enum handles: Init, Tick, InputChanged, Eval
- Component lifecycle:
  - `create()`: Initializes EmulatorCore, queues Init message
  - `update()` handles initialization: assembles code, loads into emulator, runs tests
  - Tick loop: runs 50,000 instruction batches, reads UART output, reschedules via gloo timers
  - Eval: sends input bytes to UART RX, resumes execution
- UI: Two-panel layout (output above, input below) with split-pane styling, inline CSS with theming

### 2. **Build Configuration**

**Cargo.toml** - Edition 2024, key dependencies:
- `cor24-emulator = { path = "../../sw-embed/cor24-rs" }` - Path dependency to assembler/emulator
- `cor24-isa = { path = "../../sw-embed/cor24-rs/isa", features = ["serde"] }` - ISA definitions
- `yew = { version = "0.21", features = ["csr"] }` - Client-side rendering
- `wasm-bindgen = "0.2"` - WASM FFI
- `web-sys` - DOM/console access
- `gloo = "0.11"` - Web utilities (timers, storage, net)
- `console_error_panic_hook` - Error reporting

**Trunk.toml:**
- Output directory: `pages` (dist folder for built artifacts)
- Dev server port: 7402

**index.html:**
- Catppuccin dark theme color scheme (mocha palette)
- Monospace font stack (JetBrains Mono, Fira Code, Cascadia Code)
- Root `#app` div for Yew to mount
- Includes `<link data-trunk rel="rust" data-bin="web-tml24c" />` for Trunk

**Build Status:** ✅ Both `cargo check` and `trunk build` complete successfully. The WASM bundle exists in `/Users/mike/github/sw-vibe-coding/web-tml24c/pages/` (5.1 MB WASM, 48 KB JS, 2.2 KB HTML).

### 3. **Architecture Documentation**

**/Users/mike/github/sw-vibe-coding/web-tml24c/docs/architecture.md** (74 lines) documents:

**Decision: Rust/Yew + cor24-rs library** (not Emscripten, not Rust rewrite):
- Rationale: Use cor24-rs as a library dependency; pre-compile tml24c C code to COR24 assembly via tc24r, then assemble and execute in the browser emulator
- Why not Emscripten: Defeats the purpose — users want to see Lisp running *on COR24*, not native WASM
- Why not Rust rewrite: Unnecessary; C code already compiles via tc24r

**Pipeline:**
```
Build time: tml24c (C) --[tc24r]--> tml24c.s (COR24 assembly)
Runtime: tml24c.s --[assembler]--> binary --[emulator]--> running CPU
         User input --> UART RX --> Lisp interpreter --> UART TX --> output
```

**Stack:**
- Frontend: Yew (Rust WASM framework)
- Build tool: Trunk
- CPU: cor24-emulator crate
- ISA: cor24-isa crate

**Future:** Phase 3 will use the cor24-rs `TraceBuffer` for visual execution traces.

### 4. **cor24-emulator Library Exports (cor24-rs)**

**Location:** `/Users/mike/github/sw-embed/cor24-rs/src/emulator.rs` (500+ lines)

**EmulatorCore public API:**
- **Loading:** `load_lgo()`, `load_program()`, `hard_reset()`, `reset()`
- **Execution:** `run_batch(max_instructions)` → `BatchResult { reason: StopReason, instructions_run, uart_bytes_added, led_changed }`
- **StopReason enum:** CycleLimit, Halted, InvalidInstruction, Breakpoint, Paused
- **Control:** `resume()`, `pause()`, `step()`, `step_over()`
- **Breakpoints:** `add_breakpoint()`, `remove_breakpoint()`, `clear_breakpoints()`
- **State queries:** `snapshot()` → CpuSnapshot (registers, PC, condition flag, cycle count, instruction count, LED, UART output length)
- **Memory access:** `read_byte()`, `write_byte()`, `read_word()`, `read_memory()`, `sram()`, `ebr()`
- **I/O:** `get_led()`, `set_button_pressed()`, `get_uart_output()`, `send_uart_byte()`, `clear_uart_output()`
- **Disassembly:** `disassemble_at(addr)` → (mnemonic_string, size_in_bytes)
- **Tracing:** `trace()` → TraceBuffer (last 200 instructions with register state)

**Assembler API:**
- `Assembler::new()`, `assemble(source: &str)` → `AssemblyResult`
- AssemblyResult contains: `bytes`, `lines` (with addresses and source), `errors`, `labels` (HashMap)

**CpuState:** 8 registers (r0-r2 general, r3=fp, r4=sp, r5=z, r6=iv, r7=ir), 24-bit, 16MB address space, little-endian

### 5. **tml24c C Source (Tiny Macro Lisp)**

**Location:** `/Users/mike/github/sw-vibe-coding/tml24c/src/` (1,126 lines across 8 header files + 1 C file)

**Files:**

[23..2] payload  [1:0] tag

11  Extended (heap cell, holds closures/macros/primitives/strings)

//! Demo: Blink LED
//! Toggle LED D2 on/off with a delay loop.
//! Pipeline: this file → rustc (msp430) → .msp430.s → msp430-to-cor24 → .cor24.s → assembler → emulator

#![no_std]

const LED_ADDR: u16 = 0xFF00;
const UART_DATA: u16 = 0xFF01;

#[inline(never)]
#[no_mangle]
pub unsafe fn mmio_write(addr: u16, val: u16) {
    core::ptr::write_volatile(addr as *mut u8, val as u8);
}

#[inline(never)]
#[no_mangle]
pub fn delay(mut n: u16) {
    while n != 0 {
        unsafe { core::ptr::write_volatile(&mut n as *mut u16, n - 1); }
    }
}

#[inline(never)]
#[no_mangle]
pub unsafe fn uart_putc(ch: u16) {
    mmio_write(UART_DATA, ch);
}

#[no_mangle]
pub unsafe fn demo_blinky() -> ! {
    loop {
        mmio_write(LED_ADDR, 1);   // LED on
        delay(5000);
        mmio_write(LED_ADDR, 0);   // LED off
        delay(5000);
    }
}

/// Entry point: called via reset vector (`la r0, start` + `jmp (r0)` at address 0)
#[inline(never)]
#[no_mangle]
pub unsafe fn start() -> ! {
    demo_blinky()
}

#[panic_handler]
fn panic(_: &core::panic::PanicInfo) -> ! {
    unsafe {
        uart_putc(b'P' as u16);
        uart_putc(b'A' as u16);
        uart_putc(b'N' as u16);
        uart_putc(b'I' as u16);
        uart_putc(b'C' as u16);
        uart_putc(b'\n' as u16);
    }
    loop {}
}

/* Fibonacci — from MakerLisp's COR24 C compiler */
/* Compiled with: cc24 fib.c → as24 fib.s → COR24 assembly */

int fib(int n)
{
    if (n < 2)
        return 1;

    return fib(n - 1) + fib(n - 2);
}

int main()
{
    int result;

    printf("Fibonacci 10\n");

    result = fib(10);

    printf("%d\n", result);

    return 0;
}

Challenges
Available Challenges
Load Challenge 1
Load and Add
Load the value 10 into r0, then add 5 to it. Result should be 15 in r0.
Hint:
Use 'lc r0,10' to load 10, then 'add r0,5' to add 5
Load Challenge 2
Compare and Branch
Set r0 to 1 if 5 < 10 (signed), otherwise 0. Use cls and brt/brf.
Hint:
Load values, use cls to compare, then mov r0,c to get the result
Load Challenge 3
Stack Operations
Push values 1, 2, 3 onto the stack, then pop them into r0, r1, r2.
Hint:
Remember LIFO order - last pushed is first popped
Load Challenge 4
Max of Two
Set r0 to the maximum of r0=7 and r1=12 (without branching). Use mov ra,c!
Hint:
cls sets C if r0 < r1. If true, you want r1. Use sub/add with C flag.
Load Challenge 5
Byte Sign Extension
Load -50 (0xCE) as unsigned into r0, then sign-extend it. Result should be 0xFFFFCE.
Hint:
Use lcu to load unsigned, then sxt to sign extend

COR24 Instruction Set Reference
C-Oriented RISC, 24-bit. 32 opcodes, 211 instruction forms (1, 2, or 4 bytes).
See
makerlisp.com
for the hardware specification.
CPU State
All registers and addresses are
24 bits
wide (values 0 to 16,777,215).
State
Description
PC
Program counter — address of next instruction. Starts at 0.
C
Condition flag — single bit, set by compare instructions (ceq, clu, cls), tested by branches (brt, brf). Also writable via
mov ra, c
and
clu z, ra
.
Registers
Register
Name
Width
Purpose
r0
24-bit
General purpose
r1
24-bit
General purpose / return address (jal convention)
r2
24-bit
General purpose
fp
24-bit
Frame pointer — base for stack-frame locals
sp
24-bit
Stack pointer — init 0xFEEC00, grows downward (3 bytes per push)
z
24-bit
Hardwired to zero. Readable in compares:
ceq r0, z
iv
24-bit
Interrupt vector — address of interrupt service routine
ir
24-bit
Interrupt return — saved PC when interrupt fires. Return with
jmp (ir)
Only
r0, r1, r2
can be destinations for most ALU/load instructions.
fp
can be pushed/popped and used as a memory base register.
sp
is modified by push, pop, and
sub sp
.

Pattern

Interrupts
COR24 supports one interrupt source: UART RX data ready.
Step
Action
Setup
Load ISR address into
iv
:
la r0, isr
then
mov iv, r0
Enable
Write 1 to interrupt enable register at 0xFF0010
Trigger
When UART receives a byte, CPU saves PC to
ir
and jumps to
iv
ISR body
Save registers (push), read UART data (acknowledges interrupt), process, restore (pop)
Return
jmp (ir)
— resumes execution at the interrupted instruction
Interrupts do not nest — a second interrupt cannot fire while an ISR is running.
Reading the UART data register at 0xFF0100 acknowledges the interrupt.

3 KB on MachXO FPGA; used for stack

Top of 3 KB EBR stack

Write bit 0 = LED D2. Read bit 0 = button S2

Write bit 0 = enable UART RX interrupt

Write = TX. Read = RX (acknowledges interrupt)

UART status
Bit 7 = TX busy. Bit 1 = RX data ready

; comment
Numbers: decimal (42), hex (0x2A)
Register names: r0, r1, r2, fp, sp, z, iv, ir
Calling Convention:
; Function prologue
push    fp          ; Save frame pointer
push    r2          ; Save callee-saved
push    r1          ; Save return address
mov     fp,sp       ; Set up frame

; Function body...

; Function epilogue
mov     sp,fp       ; Restore stack
pop     r1          ; Restore r1
pop     r2          ; Restore r2
pop     fp          ; Restore fp
jmp     (r1)        ; Return
Debugging Tips:
Use "Step" to execute one instruction at a time
Watch registers to see value changes (highlighted)
Check the memory viewer to see your program
The condition flag C is shown in the legend

Rust Source
Blink LED
//! Demo: Blink LED
//! Toggle LED D2 on/off with a delay loop.
//! Pipeline: this file → rustc (msp430) → .msp430.s → msp430-to-cor24 → .cor24.s → assembler → emulator

#![no_std]

const LED_ADDR: u16 = 0xFF00;
const UART_DATA: u16 = 0xFF01;

#[inline(never)]
#[no_mangle]
pub unsafe fn mmio_write(addr: u16, val: u16) {
    core::ptr::write_volatile(addr as *mut u8, val as u8);
}

#[inline(never)]
#[no_mangle]
pub fn delay(mut n: u16) {
    while n != 0 {
        unsafe { core::ptr::write_volatile(&mut n as *mut u16, n - 1); }
    }
}

#[inline(never)]
#[no_mangle]
pub unsafe fn uart_putc(ch: u16) {
    mmio_write(UART_DATA, ch);
}

#[no_mangle]
pub unsafe fn demo_blinky() -> ! {
    loop {
        mmio_write(LED_ADDR, 1);   // LED on
        delay(5000);
        mmio_write(LED_ADDR, 0);   // LED off
        delay(5000);
    }
}

/// Entry point: called via reset vector (`la r0, start` + `jmp (r0)` at address 0)
#[inline(never)]
#[no_mangle]
pub unsafe fn start() -> ! {
    demo_blinky()
}

#[panic_handler]
fn panic(_: &core::panic::PanicInfo) -> ! {
    unsafe {
        uart_putc(b'P' as u16);
        uart_putc(b'A' as u16);
        uart_putc(b'N' as u16);
        uart_putc(b'I' as u16);
        uart_putc(b'C' as u16);
        uart_putc(b'\n' as u16);
    }
    loop {}
}
Pipeline: Rust → rustc (msp430-none-elf) → MSP430 ASM → COR24 ASM → Machine Code. Examples are pre-built.

Challenges
Available Challenges
Load Challenge 1
Load and Add
Load the value 10 into r0, then add 5 to it. Result should be 15 in r0.
Hint:
Use 'lc r0,10' to load 10, then 'add r0,5' to add 5
Load Challenge 2
Compare and Branch
Set r0 to 1 if 5 < 10 (signed), otherwise 0. Use cls and brt/brf.
Hint:
Load values, use cls to compare, then mov r0,c to get the result
Load Challenge 3
Stack Operations
Push values 1, 2, 3 onto the stack, then pop them into r0, r1, r2.
Hint:
Remember LIFO order - last pushed is first popped
Load Challenge 4
Max of Two
Set r0 to the maximum of r0=7 and r1=12 (without branching). Use mov ra,c!
Hint:
cls sets C if r0 < r1. If true, you want r1. Use sub/add with C flag.
Load Challenge 5
Byte Sign Extension
Load -50 (0xCE) as unsigned into r0, then sign-extend it. Result should be 0xFFFFCE.
Hint:
Use lcu to load unsigned, then sxt to sign extend

C-Oriented RISC, 24-bit. 32 opcodes, 211 instruction forms (1, 2, or 4 bytes).

makerlisp.com
for the hardware specification.

All registers and addresses are
24 bits
wide (values 0 to 16,777,215).
State
Description
PC
Program counter — address of next instruction. Starts at 0.
C
Condition flag — single bit, set by compare instructions (ceq, clu, cls), tested by branches (brt, brf). Also writable via
mov ra, c
and
clu z, ra
.

Interrupts
COR24 supports one interrupt source: UART RX data ready.
Step
Action
Setup
Load ISR address into
iv
:
la r0, isr
then
mov iv, r0
Enable
Write 1 to interrupt enable register at 0xFF0010
Trigger
When UART receives a byte, CPU saves PC to
ir
and jumps to
iv
ISR body
Save registers (push), read UART data (acknowledges interrupt), process, restore (pop)
Return
jmp (ir)
— resumes execution at the interrupted instruction
Interrupts do not nest — a second interrupt cannot fire while an ISR is running.
Reading the UART data register at 0xFF0100 acknowledges the interrupt.

pop     r2          ; Restore r2

Blink LED
//! Demo: Blink LED
//! Toggle LED D2 on/off with a delay loop.
//! Pipeline: this file → rustc (msp430) → .msp430.s → msp430-to-cor24 → .cor24.s → assembler → emulator

#![no_std]

const LED_ADDR: u16 = 0xFF00;
const UART_DATA: u16 = 0xFF01;

#[inline(never)]
#[no_mangle]
pub unsafe fn mmio_write(addr: u16, val: u16) {
    core::ptr::write_volatile(addr as *mut u8, val as u8);
}

#[inline(never)]
#[no_mangle]
pub fn delay(mut n: u16) {
    while n != 0 {
        unsafe { core::ptr::write_volatile(&mut n as *mut u16, n - 1); }
    }
}

#[inline(never)]
#[no_mangle]
pub unsafe fn uart_putc(ch: u16) {
    mmio_write(UART_DATA, ch);
}

#[no_mangle]
pub unsafe fn demo_blinky() -> ! {
    loop {
        mmio_write(LED_ADDR, 1);   // LED on
        delay(5000);
        mmio_write(LED_ADDR, 0);   // LED off
        delay(5000);
    }
}

/// Entry point: called via reset vector (`la r0, start` + `jmp (r0)` at address 0)
#[inline(never)]
#[no_mangle]
pub unsafe fn start() -> ! {
    demo_blinky()
}

#[panic_handler]
fn panic(_: &core::panic::PanicInfo) -> ! {
    unsafe {
        uart_putc(b'P' as u16);
        uart_putc(b'A' as u16);
        uart_putc(b'N' as u16);
        uart_putc(b'I' as u16);
        uart_putc(b'C' as u16);
        uart_putc(b'\n' as u16);
    }
    loop {}
}

/* Fibonacci — from MakerLisp's COR24 C compiler */
/* Compiled with: cc24 fib.c → as24 fib.s → COR24 assembly */

int fib(int n)
{
    if (n < 2)
        return 1;

    return fib(n - 1) + fib(n - 2);
}

int main()
{
    int result;

    printf("Fibonacci 10\n");

    result = fib(10);

    printf("%d\n", result);

    return 0;
}

Tutorial
COR24 Assembly Tutorial
What is the COR24?
The COR24 is a 24-bit RISC processor designed by MakerLisp for the
MakerLisp
project.
It runs as a soft CPU on Lattice MachXO FPGAs. This emulator lets you write,
assemble, and step through COR24 code in your browser.
Getting Started
Load an example from the
Examples
button
Click
Assemble
to convert to machine code
Click
Step
to execute one instruction (watch registers change)
Click
Run
for continuous execution (
Stop
to pause)
Expand
Instruction Trace
to see recent execution history
Registers
r0, r1, r2
General purpose (24-bit)
fp
Frame pointer
sp (r4)
Stack pointer (init: 0xFEEC00, grows down)
z
Always zero — use in compares:
ceq r0,z
iv (r6)
Interrupt vector (ISR address)
ir (r7)
Interrupt return address
Loading Constants
lc  r0, 42      ; signed 8-bit (-128..127)
lcu r0, 200     ; unsigned 8-bit (0..255)
la  r0, 1000    ; 24-bit immediate (any value)
Arithmetic
add r0, r1      ; r0 = r0 + r1
add r0, 5       ; r0 = r0 + 5 (8-bit immediate)
sub r0, r1      ; r0 = r0 - r1
mul r0, r1      ; r0 = r0 * r1
sub sp, 12      ; allocate stack space
Logic & Shifts
and r0, r1      ; bitwise AND
or  r0, r1      ; bitwise OR
xor r0, r1      ; bitwise XOR
shl r0, r1      ; shift left by r1
srl r0, r1      ; shift right (zero fill)
sra r0, r1      ; shift right (sign fill)
Compare & Branch
Compares set the condition flag
C
. Branches test it.
ceq r0, r1      ; C = (r0 == r1)
clu r0, r1      ; C = (r0 < r1) unsigned
cls r0, r1      ; C = (r0 < r1) signed
bra label       ; branch always
brt label       ; branch if C = true
brf label       ; branch if C = false
Example — loop while r0 < 10:
lc  r0, 0
loop:
        add r0, 1
        lc  r1, 10
        clu r0, r1      ; C = (r0 < 10)
        brt loop
Halting
No halt instruction — use branch-to-self:
halt:
        bra halt         ; emulator detects this
Register Moves
mov r0, r1      ; copy register
mov r0, c       ; r0 = condition flag (0 or 1)
mov fp, sp      ; save stack pointer
mov iv, r0      ; set interrupt vector
Memory Access
Base+offset addressing. Offset is signed 8-bit. Base: r0, r1, r2, or fp.
sb  r0, 0(r1)   ; store byte
sw  r0, 0(r1)   ; store word (3 bytes)
lb  r0, 0(r1)   ; load byte (sign-extended)
lbu r0, 0(r1)   ; load byte (zero-extended)
lw  r0, 0(r1)   ; load word (3 bytes)

Interrupts
COR24 supports one interrupt source: UART RX data ready.
Step
Action
Setup
Load ISR address into
iv

then
mov iv, r0
Enable
Write 1 to interrupt enable register at 0xFF0010
Trigger
When UART receives a byte, CPU saves PC to
ir
and jumps to
iv
ISR body
Save registers (push), read UART data (acknowledges interrupt), process, restore (pop)
Return
jmp (ir)
— resumes execution at the interrupted instruction
Interrupts do not nest — a second interrupt cannot fire while an ISR is running.
Reading the UART data register at 0xFF0100 acknowledges the interrupt.

Blink LED
//! Demo: Blink LED
//! Toggle LED D2 on/off with a delay loop.
//! Pipeline: this file → rustc (msp430) → .msp430.s → msp430-to-cor24 → .cor24.s → assembler → emulator

#![no_std]

const LED_ADDR: u16 = 0xFF00;
const UART_DATA: u16 = 0xFF01;

#[inline(never)]
#[no_mangle]
pub unsafe fn mmio_write(addr: u16, val: u16) {
    core::ptr::write_volatile(addr as *mut u8, val as u8);
}

#[inline(never)]
#[no_mangle]
pub fn delay(mut n: u16) {
    while n != 0 {
        unsafe { core::ptr::write_volatile(&mut n as *mut u16, n - 1); }
    }
}

#[inline(never)]
#[no_mangle]
pub unsafe fn uart_putc(ch: u16) {
    mmio_write(UART_DATA, ch);
}

#[no_mangle]
pub unsafe fn demo_blinky() -> ! {
    loop {
        mmio_write(LED_ADDR, 1);   // LED on
        delay(5000);
        mmio_write(LED_ADDR, 0);   // LED off
        delay(5000);
    }
}

/// Entry point: called via reset vector (`la r0, start` + `jmp (r0)` at address 0)
#[inline(never)]
#[no_mangle]
pub unsafe fn start() -> ! {
    demo_blinky()
}

#[panic_handler]
fn panic(_: &core::panic::PanicInfo) -> ! {
    unsafe {
        uart_putc(b'P' as u16);
        uart_putc(b'A' as u16);
        uart_putc(b'N' as u16);
        uart_putc(b'I' as u16);
        uart_putc(b'C' as u16);
        uart_putc(b'\n' as u16);
    }
    loop {}
}
Pipeline: Rust → rustc (msp430-none-elf) → MSP430 ASM → COR24 ASM → Machine Code. Examples are pre-built.

/* Fibonacci — from MakerLisp's COR24 C compiler */
/* Compiled with: cc24 fib.c → as24 fib.s → COR24 assembly */

int fib(int n)
{
    if (n < 2)
        return 1;

    return fib(n - 1) + fib(n - 2);
}

int main()
{
    int result;

    printf("Fibonacci 10\n");

    result = fib(10);

    printf("%d\n", result);

    return 0;
}

What is the COR24?
The COR24 is a 24-bit RISC processor designed by MakerLisp for the
MakerLisp
project.
It runs as a soft CPU on Lattice MachXO FPGAs. This emulator lets you write,
assemble, and step through COR24 code in your browser.

1

Use

register for zero tests:

sets C if r0 == 0.

Instruction
Bytes
Description

Instruction
Bytes
Description

2
Load byte, zero-extend to 24 bits

2
Store byte (low 8 bits of ra)

Valid base registers: r0, r1, r2, fp. (Not sp — use

then fp.)
Stack
Instruction
Bytes
Description

1
sp -= 3; store ra at sp (word)

1
Load ra from sp; sp += 3 (word)
Can push/pop: r0, r1, r2, fp.
Jump & Call
Instruction
Bytes
Description

1
PC = ra (unconditional jump)

1
ra = return addr; PC = rb (jump and link)
Register Move

Extensions
Instruction
Bytes
Description

Sign-extend byte: bits 8..23 = bit 7

Zero-extend byte: bits 8..23 = 0
nop
1
No operation (0xFF)
Idioms
Pattern
Meaning
halt:

Halt (branch-to-self; emulator detects this)
la  r2, func
        jal r1, (r2)
Call function (r1 = return address)
jmp (r1)
Return from function
jmp (ir)
Return from interrupt
Interrupts
COR24 supports one interrupt source: UART RX data ready.
Step
Action
Setup
Load ISR address into
iv
:
la r0, isr
then
mov iv, r0
Enable
Write 1 to interrupt enable register at 0xFF0010
Trigger
When UART receives a byte, CPU saves PC to
ir
and jumps to
iv
ISR body
Save registers (push), read UART data (acknowledges interrupt), process, restore (pop)
Return
jmp (ir)
— resumes execution at the interrupted instruction
Interrupts do not nest — a second interrupt cannot fire while an ISR is running.
Reading the UART data register at 0xFF0100 acknowledges the interrupt.

Address Range
Region
Notes
000000-0FFFFF

Code at low addresses, data/globals above

FF0010

FF0100
UART data

FF0101
UART status

content.text: Good, now it recompiled. Let me also check what changed in `getc_uart` - the user fixed it to check bit 0 instead of bit 1. Let me also check if the UART status bit mapping is correct for the emulator.

Good - the emulator uses bit 0 for RX ready, which matches the updated `getc_uart`. But there's an important issue: `send_uart_byte` in the emulator can only buffer ONE byte at a time (setting `uart_rx_ready = true` for one byte). The REPL sends the entire input line at once, but the CPU can only read one byte at a time. Each `send_uart_byte` call overwrites the previous if the CPU hasn't read it yet!

Let me check how `send_uart_byte` on `EmulatorCore` works vs the raw `uart_send_rx`.

`send_uart_byte` just calls `uart_send_rx` which overwrites. Sending a whole line at once will lose characters. I need to send bytes one at a time, with the CPU running between each byte to read it.

Let me redesign the Eval handler to use a buffered approach - store the pending input, and feed one byte at a time during tick processing when the CPU is polling for input.
